Keywords: impact, feasibility, quick-win, effort, value, 2x2, prioritization
Description: Plot issues on a 2x2 matrix: Impact (high/low) vs Feasibility (high/low). Focus on high-impact high-feasibility first (quick wins), then high-impact low-feasibility (big bets).
When to Use: Initial screening of logic tree branches; Resource allocation; Project portfolio prioritization; When you have many options and limited resources
How to Apply: List all issues/options; Rate each on impact (1-10) and feasibility (1-10); Plot on 2x2 grid; Quadrants: Quick Wins (high/high), Big Bets (high/low), Fill-ins (low/high), Deprioritize (low/low)
Output Format: 2x2 matrix with items plotted; Ordered priority list; Clear rationale for each rating
Pitfalls: Spending too long on ratings; Not aligning on rating criteria upfront; Confusing activity with impact; Ignoring interdependencies between items

Keywords: sensitivity, driver, leverage, impact, which-matters-most, tornado
Description: Test which variables have the most impact on the outcome. Vary each factor independently and measure effect on the final answer. Focus analysis on high-sensitivity factors.
When to Use: After building a model or logic tree; When deciding where to focus analysis; When building a business case; Resource allocation decisions
How to Apply: Build base case model; Vary each input by fixed % (e.g., +/- 20%); Measure impact on output; Rank factors by sensitivity; Focus analysis on top 3-5 most sensitive factors
Output Format: Tornado diagram (bars showing impact range for each factor); Ranked list of sensitivities; Recommended analysis priorities
Pitfalls: Not varying enough factors; Using unrealistic ranges; Ignoring correlations between factors; Not updating sensitivities as you learn more

Keywords: voting, team, consensus, democratic, rapid, alignment, group
Description: Each team member gets a fixed number of votes (dots) to allocate across options. Quick way to surface team priorities and build alignment.
When to Use: Team workshops; When you need rapid consensus; When individual expertise varies; Brainstorming session follow-up; When debate would take too long
How to Apply: Give each person N dots (typically 3-5); Members place dots on items they think are most important; Count votes; Discuss any surprising results; Use as input to formal prioritization
Output Format: Ranked list of items by vote count; Heat map of team priorities; Consensus vs divergence view
Pitfalls: Groupthink (voting publicly); Too many dots dilutes signal; Not discussing 'why' behind votes; Treating it as final decision vs input

Description: Identify the vital few factors that account for the majority of the outcome. In most situations, roughly 20% of causes produce 80% of effects.
When to Use: When dealing with many potential causes; Resource allocation; Focus identification; When data shows distribution patterns; Portfolio analysis
How to Apply: Gather data on all factors and their contributions; Sort by contribution (descending); Calculate cumulative percentage; Draw the 80/20 line; Focus on factors above the line
Output Format: Pareto chart (bars + cumulative line); Vital few list; Recommended focus areas
Pitfalls: Assuming exact 80/20 split; Not having good data on contributions; Ignoring the 'trivial many' entirely (some may become vital); Confusing frequency with impact

Keywords: urgent, important, time-management, triage, delegation, planning
Description: Classify issues by Urgency (high/low) and Importance (high/low). Do urgent+important now, schedule important but not urgent, delegate urgent but not important, eliminate the rest.
When to Use: Personal or team task management; When overwhelmed with issues; Daily/weekly planning; When urgent displaces important work; Crisis vs strategic work balancing
How to Apply: List all issues/tasks; Rate each on urgency and importance; Place in 4 quadrants: Do (urgent+important), Schedule (important), Delegate (urgent), Eliminate (neither); Act accordingly
Output Format: 2x2 matrix; Action plan for each quadrant; Protected time for Quadrant 2 (important not urgent)
Pitfalls: Everything feels urgent; Not being honest about importance vs preference; Spending all time in Do quadrant; Not actually scheduling Quadrant 2 time

Keywords: criteria, scoring, weighted, multi-criteria, decision-matrix, comparison
Description: Score options against multiple criteria with different weights. Provides structured comparison when multiple factors matter and not all are equally important.
When to Use: Comparing multiple options; When stakeholders value different criteria; Technology selection; Vendor evaluation; Location decisions; Any multi-criteria decision
How to Apply: Define criteria; Assign weights (must sum to 100%); Score each option on each criterion (1-10); Calculate weighted scores; Review and sanity-check total scores; Discuss surprises
Output Format: Decision matrix table; Weighted scores per option; Sensitivity analysis on weights; Recommended option with rationale
Pitfalls: Garbage in garbage out (bad criteria or scores); Anchoring on first option scored; Not testing sensitivity to weight changes; Using scores to avoid making a judgment call

Keywords: decision, probability, expected-value, options, outcomes, risk, branches
Description: Map out sequential decisions and uncertain outcomes as a tree. Calculate expected value at each branch to identify optimal strategy under uncertainty.
When to Use: Investment decisions; Go/no-go decisions; Strategy under uncertainty; When outcomes depend on sequential choices; Risk quantification
How to Apply: Draw decision nodes (squares) and chance nodes (circles); Assign probabilities to chance branches; Assign values to endpoints; Calculate expected values working backward; Choose highest EV branch
Output Format: Decision tree diagram; Expected values per path; Optimal strategy recommendation; Risk profile
Pitfalls: Overconfidence in probability estimates; Missing important branches; Ignoring option value (flexibility); Not updating probabilities with new information; Optimizing one decision in isolation

Keywords: must-have, should-have, could-have, wont-have, scope, requirements, MVP
Description: Classify requirements into Must have, Should have, Could have, Wont have. Forces clear scoping and prevents scope creep.
When to Use: Product planning; Project scoping; Feature prioritization; When stakeholders have competing demands; MVP definition; Sprint planning
How to Apply: List all requirements/features; Classify each as M/S/C/W with rationale; Validate Must-haves are truly essential; Ensure Should-haves are feasible within constraints; Get stakeholder sign-off
Output Format: Prioritized requirements list; Scope boundary document; Stakeholder alignment on cuts; Clear rationale for each classification
Pitfalls: Too many Must-haves; Not being ruthless enough; Stakeholder negotiation turning into all Must-haves; Not revisiting classification as conditions change
